I am an AI assistant for CS/AI topics. You can call me Bot.
I'm a learning chatbot built with FastAPI, sklearn and spaCy.
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the field of building machines that can perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as perception, reasoning, and language understanding.
Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI focused on building systems that learn from data. AI is broader and includes rule-based systems, planning and reasoning as well.
Deep Learning uses multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations from data. Popular architectures include CNNs for images and Transformers for language and multimodal tasks.

NLP (Natural Language Processing) is the field of enabling machines to understand and generate human language. Tasks include tokenization, NER, POS tagging, parsing, summarization, translation and question answering.
Transformers are models that rely on self-attention to model relationships between tokens. They replaced RNNs for many NLP tasks and form the basis of BERT, GPT and many other models.

BERT is a bidirectional encoder suitable for understanding tasks (classification, NER). GPT is an autoregressive decoder optimized for text generation. Each has different pretraining objectives and use-cases.
Prompt engineering is designing input prompts to guide large language models. Use clear instructions, few-shot examples, and explicit output formats. For best results iterate and include constraints.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) combines a retriever (vector DB or search) with a generator (LLM). Retrieve relevant documents, then condition the LLM on them for more accurate responses and up-to-date info.
Vector databases store embeddings and enable nearest-neighbor search for semantic retrieval. Popular options: FAISS, Milvus, Pinecone, Weaviate. They're key for RAG and semantic search.
Embeddings are numeric vector representations of text (or images) capturing semantic information. Use models like SBERT, OpenAI embeddings, or CLIP for multimodal embeddings.
Computer Vision (CV) enables machines to interpret images and video. Tasks include image classification, object detection (bounding boxes), and segmentation (pixel-level labels).
YOLO is a family of real-time object detectors optimized for speed and good accuracy. Faster R-CNN is more accurate in some settings but slower. YOLOv5/YOLOv8 are commonly used for production.
Vision Transformers (ViT) apply transformer architectures to image patches. They can achieve strong performance especially with large-scale pretraining and data augmentation.
Diffusion models are generative models that learn to reverse a noising process to generate samples. They power image-generation systems like Stable Diffusion and DALL·E 2 (partially).
Generative AI creates new data (images, text, audio). Examples: GANs, VAEs, diffusion models, and large generative language models. Each has tradeoffs in quality and training stability.

Self-supervised learning uses intrinsic structure of data to create supervision signals (e.g., predicting parts of input). Contrastive learning (SimCLR) trains models to bring similar views closer in embedding space.
Transfer learning reuses a pretrained model and fine-tunes it on a new task. Helpful when you have limited labeled data; commonly used in CV and NLP.
Reinforcement Learning trains agents via rewards and interactions with an environment. Algorithms include Q-learning, DQN, PPO and actor-critic methods. Useful for sequential decision-making.
Federated Learning trains models across multiple devices or silos without moving raw data to a central server — useful for privacy-preserving ML.
MLOps combines ML engineering and software practices: automated training, CI/CD, model serving, monitoring, data versioning, and handling model drift in production.

Accuracy is overall correct predictions; Precision measures positives that are true; Recall measures how many true positives were found. F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. Use ROC AUC for ranking tasks.
Explainability methods (SHAP, LIME) help understand model predictions by attributing importance to features. Useful for debugging, fairness and regulatory reasons.
Containerize models with Docker to ensure environment consistency. Build an image with your app and dependencies, then run containers in production with orchestration tools like Kubernetes.
Edge AI runs models on devices (phones, cameras) for low-latency and privacy. Tools: TensorFlow Lite, ONNX, model quantization and pruning for smaller footprints.
Start with Python basics (syntax, data types, control flow), then learn libraries like NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, and frameworks like scikit-learn and PyTorch/TensorFlow for ML.
Good resources: 'Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras & TensorFlow' by A. Géron; 'Deep Learning' by Goodfellow; online courses from Coursera, fast.ai and CS programs on edX.
I'm sorry — I don't understand. Could you rephrase or ask about AI, ML, Python or CS topics?
